content: I've been trying to get back into cooking since my knee injury has kept me home more. It's been a challenge though - standing for long periods is still difficult.
I'm sorry to hear about your injury, Spencer. Have you found any kitchen modifications or techniques that make cooking easier while you recover?
I've started using a tall stool at the counter so I can sit while prepping. Also been focusing on one-pot meals since they require less movement around the kitchen.
Those are great adaptations! One-pot meals are perfect for this. Do you have any favorite recipes you've been making?
I've been making a lot of hearty soups and stews. My chicken and wild rice soup has been a go-to. I used to make it after long days at the clinic, but now it's more of a weekend project.
That sounds perfect for recovery - nutritious and comforting. How long have you been a veterinarian?
Almost 30 years now. I run a small rural practice about 30 miles outside of Omaha. Most of my patients are farm animals, though I still see plenty of dogs and cats too.
That's quite a career! I imagine your work keeps you very active. How has the transition to being more homebound been for you?

It's been an adjustment. I miss the daily physical activity, but I've been trying to stay engaged. I've actually gotten back into knitting - something I haven't done since my wife was alive. She taught me when we were first married.
That's a beautiful way to honor her memory and stay productive. What kinds of things do you knit?
Mostly practical things - scarves, hats, dishcloths. My daughter just had a baby, so I've been making little booties and blankets. It's nice to have something to show for my time.
That's wonderful - handmade gifts are so special. Do you listen to anything while you knit?
I've become a bit of a podcast addict actually. I listen to everything from true crime to veterinary medicine updates. It helps pass the time while I'm recovering.
Podcasts are perfect for that. Any recommendations for someone new to the medium?
